title: Wet-felted wine bottle cover
subtitle: A cylindrical merino sleeve to fit a standard 750 ml bottle, wet-felted over a plastic bottle resist
excerpt: A snug merino sleeve formed over a plastic-bottle resist, wet-felted and fulled to shape around a 750 ml wine bottle. The resist is removed before finishing to leave a clean hollow cylinder.
sourceNotes: Wet-felting over a plastic bottle is a standard beginner-level resist project used in UK and European felting classes. Technique synthesised from general wet-felting and resist-felting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A form placed inside the roving layers during wet felting to prevent the fibres from bonding in a particular area, leaving a hollow or opening in the finished piece.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, used as the raw material for wet felting.
The final stage of wet felting in which the fabric is compressed and shrunk by vigorous agitation, producing a dense, firm felt.

content.text: A wet-felted bottle cover is a
resist
project built around a standard 750 ml plastic drinks bottle. The
roving

is laid in cross-hatch layers over the bottle, wetted, and worked until the fibres begin to bond around the form. Once the felt is stable, the plastic bottle is removed, and the open end is trimmed to a neat edge. The result is a cylindrical sleeve that fits a wine or olive oil bottle snugly, protecting it during storage or making it a presentable gift wrapper.

attrs.title: Materials needed
attrs.body: Use a 750 ml plastic drinks bottle as the resist. A washed, label-free bottle works well. Merino roving at 19-21 micron gives the smoothest finish.

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the resist

Remove the label from a clean 750 ml plastic bottle and fill it with water so it has some weight and does not roll. The filled bottle acts as a stable form to work around. Lay the felting mat on a flat surface.
Step 2: Lay the roving layers

Pull thin wisps of roving and lay them around the bottle in a vertical direction, covering the lower two-thirds of the bottle completely. Add a second layer at 90 degrees, running horizontally around the circumference. Add a third vertical layer and a fourth horizontal layer. Each layer should be thin and even. The roving should extend approximately 2 cm beyond the base of the bottle to allow for shrinkage.
Step 3: Wet down and begin felting

Mix a few drops of olive-oil soap into warm water (around 45°C). Pour the soapy water slowly over the roving-wrapped bottle until every layer is saturated. Cup both hands around the bottle and squeeze gently to press the layers together and remove air. Begin rubbing the surface with flat hands in circular movements. Apply light pressure at first, increasing after 3 minutes as the fibres begin to bond.
Step 4: Roll and work the piece

Once the roving forms a surface skin and no longer slides on the bottle, wrap the bottle in the bamboo rolling mat and roll it back and forth on the table with firm pressure for 5 minutes. Unroll, rotate the bottle 90 degrees, and roll again for 5 minutes. Repeat twice more, rotating each time. Work the base by pressing it firmly against the mat with the heel of the hand.
Step 5: Remove the resist and full

When the felt is firm enough to hold its shape independently (usually after 20-25 minutes of working), empty the water from the plastic bottle and ease it out through the open top of the sleeve. The sleeve should be stable but not yet fully
fulled

. Continue working the sleeve by rolling it on the mat and throwing it against the felting mat 15 to 20 times. Turn the sleeve inside out occasionally during this stage to even the density throughout. The sleeve is fully fulled when it no longer springs back to its original size under compression.
Step 6: Trim and finish

Trim the open upper edge to a straight line with scissors. The felt is dense enough to cut cleanly without fraying. Slip the bottle back inside to check the fit: the sleeve should slip on and stay in place with slight resistance. Rinse the sleeve in cool water until the water runs clear. Squeeze out excess water without wringing. Slip the actual wine or oil bottle inside and leave to dry in position so the sleeve dries to the exact diameter.

items.problem: The roving slips off the bottle before it begins to bond
items.cause: The bottle surface is too smooth and the initial rubbing pressure too light
items.fix: Hold the layers in place with a piece of bubble wrap around the outside while applying the first few minutes of rubbing pressure. Once the fibres begin to bond, the bubble wrap can be removed.

items.problem: The sleeve is stuck to the bottle and will not release
items.cause: Felting was done too vigorously before the fibres had a chance to begin separating from the resist
items.fix: Wet the sleeve thoroughly and work a finger around the inside top edge to loosen the fibres. Pull the sleeve away from the resist slowly while re-wetting.

items.problem: The base of the sleeve is thinner than the sides
items.cause: The base was not worked as thoroughly as the sides during rolling
items.fix: Spend extra time pressing and rubbing the base on the mat before removing the resist. The base often needs additional direct pressure.

items.problem: The sleeve is too wide and does not fit the bottle snugly
items.fix: Continue rolling and throwing the sleeve until it shrinks to fit. Wet-felt shrinks further on re-wetting and re-working if not yet fully fulled.

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash the sleeve in cool water with a small amount of wool-safe detergent if needed. Dry over the bottle to maintain the shape. Do not tumble dry. The sleeve can be decorated with a needle-felted motif or wrapped with a contrasting-colour spiral of roving before felting if a two-colour design is wanted.
